bug_description: The variable 'target' is incorrectly defined as a boolean instead of an integer, leading to improper handling of numeric values and causing incorrect outputs.
problem_intro:
# C. Meximum Array 2

**Time limit:** 2 seconds
**Memory limit:** 256 megabytes

## Problem Statement

[Ferus Melek - Brute Force](https://soundcloud.com/ferusmelek/brute-force)

⠀

You are given three positive integers $$$n$$$, $$$k$$$, and $$$q$$$. You are also given $$$q$$$ tuples $$$(c, l, r)$$$, with $$$1 \\leq c \\leq 2$$$ and $$$1 \\leq l \\leq r \\leq n$$$.

An array $$$a\_1, a\_2, \\ldots, a\_n$$$ is meximum if $$$0 \\leq a\_i \\leq 10^9$$$ for each $$$i$$$ in $$$\[1, n\]$$$, and for each given tuple $$$(c, l, r)$$$,

*   if $$$c = 1$$$, then $$$\\min(a\_l, a\_{l+1}, \\ldots, a\_r) = k$$$;
*   if $$$c = 2$$$, then $$$\\operatorname{MEX}$$$$$$^{\\text{∗}}$$$$$$(a\_l, a\_{l+1}, \\ldots, a\_r) = k$$$.

Note that the parameter $$$k$$$ is the same for all the conditions.

Find a meximum array $$$a\_1, a\_2, \\ldots, a\_n$$$ of length $$$n$$$. The input is generated in such a way that a valid array always exists. If there are multiple possible arrays, you can print any one of them.

bug_description: The buggy solution incorrectly prints 'j' for cases where the MEX condition is satisfied, instead of the required value 'j % target', causing it to potentially output incorrect values.

bug_description: The buggy solution contains an off-by-one error in the condition when checking for palindromic segments. It incorrectly handles the boundaries when deciding to break out of loops and determining valid index ranges, which could lead to incorrect flipping of segments.
problem_intro:
# D. Palindrome Flipping

**Time limit:** 2 seconds
**Memory limit:** 256 megabytes

## Problem Statement

You are given two binary strings $$$s$$$ and $$$t$$$, each of the same length $$$n$$$. You are allowed to perform the following operation:

*   Pick indices $$$l$$$, $$$r$$$ ($$$1 \\le \\color{red}{l \\lt r} \\le n$$$) such that substring $$$s\_{l,r}$$$ is a palindrome and flip all bits in substring $$$s\_{l,r}$$$.

The goal is to finally make $$$s$$$ equal to $$$t$$$, performing any of the above operations at most $$$2n$$$ times (possibly none).

A substring $$$s\_{l,r}$$$ of a string $$$s$$$ is the contiguous sequence of characters starting from index $$$l$$$ and ending at index $$$r$$$ (both inclusive), where $$$1 \\leq l \\lt r \\leq |s|$$$. Here $$$|s|$$$ denotes the length of the string $$$s$$$.

A string is a palindrome if it reads the same forwards and backwards. For example, the strings 101 and 00 are palindromes, while 10 is not.

bug_description: The buggy solution does not properly handle user mentions due to a flawed conditional logic that fails to ensure all necessary elements are included in the final output sequence, leading to incorrect lexicographical ordering.
problem_intro:
# C. Simons and Posting Blogs

**Time limit:** 2 seconds
**Memory limit:** 256 megabytes

## Problem Statement

This life is like a TV show; we're swept along as the plotlines go.

— SHUN, [TRAP](https://open.spotify.com/track/4lfS48CaivAm4QaimqbAPv)

There are $$$n$$$ blogs. The $$$i$$$-th blog mentioned $$$l\_i$$$ users in order as an array $$$a\_i=\[a\_{i,1},a\_{i,2},\\ldots,a\_{i,l\_i}\]$$$.

You are going to post all $$$n$$$ blogs. Let us maintain a sequence $$$Q$$$ that describes the list of users you have recently mentioned. You need to perform the following operation exactly $$$n$$$ times:

*   Choose an unposted blog $$$i$$$ ($$$1\\le i\\le n$$$), then post it. This will cause the following operations for each $$$1\\le j\\le l\_i$$$ in order:
    *   If $$$a\_{i,j}$$$ already exists in $$$Q$$$, then move $$$a\_{i,j}$$$ to the beginning of $$$Q$$$.
    *   Otherwise, insert $$$a\_{i,j}$$$ at the beginning of $$$Q$$$.

Find the lexicographically smallest$$$^{\\text{∗}}$$$ $$$Q$$

bug_description: The buggy solution incorrectly handles the counting of fortified bases with respect to the number of soldiers available per day and their movement strategy, resulting in a wrong maximum count of fortified bases.
problem_intro:
# C. War Strategy

**Time limit:** 2 seconds
**Memory limit:** 256 megabytes

## Problem Statement

A war has broken out! You, as the country's top general, must strategize where to place your troops.

There are $$$n$$$ bases in a line, with the $$$k$$$-th of which being the home base for your army. Initially, there is only a single soldier at base $$$k$$$. Each day, the following happens in order:

*   You give out an order by choosing a base $$$i$$$ ($$$1 \\leq i \\leq n$$$), and any number of soldiers inside that base (which is allowed to be $$$0$$$ or all soldiers in that base currently). Then, tell all soldiers you ordered to either move to base $$$i-1$$$ or base $$$i+1$$$. All soldiers must move in the same direction, and no soldier is allowed to move to the left of base $$$1$$$ or to the right of base $$$n$$$.
*   Then, a new soldier moves onto base $$$k$$$. This soldier cannot be ordered by that day's commands.

However, time is tight, and there are only $$$m$$$ days until the

bug_description: The algorithm contains a flaw in its conditional logic when setting the values for the array based on the conditions. Specifically, it incorrectly calculates the values for the 'mex' condition, which leads to violating the required conditions specified in the problem statement.

# C. Meximum Array 2

**Time limit:** 2 seconds
**Memory limit:** 256 megabytes

## Problem Statement

[Ferus Melek - Brute Force](https://soundcloud.com/ferusmelek/brute-force)

⠀

You are given three positive integers $$$n$$$, $$$k$$$, and $$$q$$$. You are also given $$$q$$$ tuples $$$(c, l, r)$$$, with $$$1 \\leq c \\leq 2$$$ and $$$1 \\leq l \\leq r \\leq n$$$.

An array $$$a\_1, a\_2, \\ldots, a\_n$$$ is meximum if $$$0 \\leq a\_i \\leq 10^9$$$ for each $$$i$$$ in $$$\[1, n\]$$$, and for each given tuple $$$(c, l, r)$$$,

*   if $$$c = 1$$$, then $$$\\min(a\_l, a\_{l+1}, \\ldots, a\_r) = k$$$;
*   if $$$c = 2$$$, then $$$\\operatorname{MEX}$$$$$$^{\\text{∗}}$$$$$$(a\_l, a\_{l+1}, \\ldots, a\_r) = k$$$.

Note that the parameter $$$k$$$ is the same for all the conditions.

Find a meximum array $$$a\_1, a\_2, \\ldots, a\_n$$$ of length $$$n$$$. The input is generated in such a way that a valid array always exists. If there are multiple possible arrays, you can print any one of them.

bug_description: The buggy solution always outputs 'y' as the second value in the pair (p, q) without considering the requirement that p & q must equal 0, leading to incorrect pairs and a wrong answer.
problem_intro:
# B. Shortest Statement Ever

**Time limit:** 2 seconds
**Memory limit:** 256 megabytes

## Problem Statement

You are given two non-negative integers $$$x$$$, $$$y$$$. Find two non-negative integers $$$p$$$ and $$$q$$$ such that $$$p\\;\\&\\;q=0$$$, and $$$|x-p|+|y-q|$$$ is minimized. Here, $$$\\&$$$ denotes the [bitwise AND operation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bitwise_operation#AND).

## Input

Each test contains multiple test cases. The first line contains the number of test cases $$$t$$$ ($$$1 \\le t \\le 10^4$$$). The description of the test cases follows.

The only line of each test case contains two non-negative integers $$$x$$$ and $$$y$$$ ($$$0 \\le x,y \\lt 2^{30}$$$).

## Output

For each test case, output two non-negative integers $$$p$$$ and $$$q$$$ you found. If there are multiple pairs of $$$p$$$ and $$$q$$$ satisfying the conditions, you may output any of them.

It can be proven that under the constraints of the problem, any valid solution satisfies $$$\\max(p,q) \\

Qwen3 Coder.test:
- We need to find p and q such that p & q = 0
- And |x-p| + |y-q| is minimized

bug_description: The buggy solution incorrectly combines conditions for outputting values by using an 'or' instead of 'and' for checking requirements, leading to wrong outputs when both req_min and req_mex are true.
